dimension_notes.naming_quality: Generally good naming with domain terms (agent, channel, server). Some generic handler names like 'createInternalMessagesPostHandler' are overly mechanical. File renaming to kebab-case is a positive convention shift.
dimension_notes.logic_clarity: The BYOK creation path in POST /api/servers/{serverId}/agents has duplicated channel-assignment logic that already exists in createAgent. The long-poll loop in agents/messages GET has a correct backoff but the double-fetch pattern on ack is complex. Some event handlers use broad 'as' casts on raw payloads without validation.
dimension_notes.type_safety: Widespread use of 'as' casts on Phoenix channel payloads (raw as MessagePayload, raw as ToolCallData, etc.) in use-channel.ts with no runtime validation. Route handlers build updateData as Record<string, unknown> and cast to Prisma types. PersistMessageData accepts 'Record<string, unknown>' union which bypasses the interface. gateway-client.ts uses Record<string, unknown> for all broadcast payloads instead of typed interfaces.
dimension_notes.contract_coherence: PROTOCOL.md contracts appear to match implementation for internal APIs. The PersistMessageData interface in internal-api-client.ts is undermined by the union with Record<string, unknown>. The bootstrap route uses zod for validation while all other routes do manual validation -- inconsistent contract enforcement.
dimension_notes.error_consistency: Consistent try/catch + console.error + NextResponse.json pattern across all route handlers. However, error detail varies: some routes return 'Internal error', others 'Failed to persist message', others 'Failed to send message'. The internal-api-client throws on error while route handlers return NextResponse -- two strategies coexist at the same layer.
dimension_notes.abstraction_fitness: Abstractions are generally well-earned. The gateway-client broadcast helpers are thin wrappers but provide meaningful topic formatting. The HandlerDeps interface in use-channel.ts extracts handler groups well. The route-handlers.ts factory pattern is justified by testability needs.
dimension_notes.ai_generated_debt: Some restating comments exist (e.g., '// Encrypt the API key' above encrypt(apiKey)). The gateway-client.ts broadcast wrappers all have identical JSDoc patterns that add no value beyond the function name. The socket.ts onOpen callback has a no-op comment.
dimension_notes.high_level_elegance: Clean separation: lib/ for shared logic, api/ for routes, components/ for UI, hooks/ for state. The Go/Elixir/Next.js boundary is well-maintained. Agent auth, factory, and API safety are properly extracted shared modules.
dimension_notes.mid_level_elegance: The internal-api-client.ts consolidation is good. However, the dispatch route does broadcast-then-persist for sync responses but persist-then-broadcast for streaming -- inconsistent ordering at the seam. The chat-provider.tsx has many fetch-and-setState patterns that could use a shared fetcher abstraction.
dimension_notes.low_level_elegance: use-channel.ts handler registration is clean after extraction. However, several streaming handlers have very long single-line expressions (e.g., stream_tool_call handler line 299). The invite route's retry loop for unique code generation is straightforward.
dimension_notes.cross_module_architecture: Good dependency direction: routes depend on lib/, components depend on hooks/. The reactions.ts module appropriately extracts shared logic used by both channel and DM reaction routes. No circular dependencies observed.
dimension_notes.initialization_coupling: The socket.ts singleton is appropriately lazy (created on first getSocket() call). The db.ts global Prisma singleton follows standard Next.js patterns. Environment reads happen at call time in internal-auth.ts and gateway-client.ts, not import time.
dimension_notes.convention_outlier: File naming migration from PascalCase to kebab-case is in progress but incomplete -- git status shows renames alongside modified files. The bootstrap route uses zod validation while all other routes use manual validation. The invites route has an inline type annotation on the map callback that other routes avoid.
dimension_notes.dependency_health: Clean dependency set. zod is included but only used in the bootstrap route. No duplicate libraries for the same purpose. No heavy unused dependencies visible.
dimension_notes.test_strategy: 25 test files exist covering auth, safety, route handlers, and utility functions. However, no tests exist for use-channel.ts (the most complex client-side module), chat-provider.tsx, or any React components. The gateway-client.ts (high fan-out, called by many routes) has zero test coverage. E2E tests exist but unit coverage of critical paths has gaps.
dimension_notes.api_surface_coherence: The v1 agent API (GET/PATCH/DELETE) uses authenticateAgentById while agent messages and webhooks use authenticateAgentRequest -- two different auth functions for the same API surface. Response shapes vary: some return {success: true}, others return the entity, others return {ok: true, mode: ...}. The DELETE /api/v1/webhooks takes webhookId as a query param while POST takes it in the body -- inconsistent parameter placement.
dimension_notes.authorization_consistency: Auth is consistently applied across routes. Internal routes use validateInternalSecret, v1 routes use agent auth, UI routes use getServerSession. The POST /api/servers/{serverId}/agents does not validate the request body JSON parse failure (line 116 has no try/catch around request.json()).
dimension_notes.incomplete_migration: Git status shows deleted .js files (api-safety.js, route-handlers.js, validation.js, validation.test.mjs) alongside new .ts replacements -- the JS-to-TS migration is complete but old files are staged for deletion. Several .test.mjs files are deleted and replaced with .test.ts equivalents. The PascalCase to kebab-case file rename is partially complete in typed-messages/.
dimension_notes.package_organization: Clean structure. lib/ contains shared logic, hooks/ in lib/ for React hooks, components organized by domain (chat/, layout/, modals/, etc.). The reactions.ts in lib/ is appropriately placed as shared logic. The internal-api-client.ts consolidation reduces duplication.
dimension_notes.design_coherence: The BYOK agent creation path in agents/route.ts POST duplicates channel-assignment logic that createAgent already handles in a transaction. The chat-provider.tsx has 5 independent refresh functions with identical fetch-setState patterns. The route-handlers.ts factory functions mix multiple concerns (auth + validation + business logic + response formatting).

issues.summary: All Phoenix channel event handlers in use-channel.ts cast raw payloads with 'as' without any runtime validation, trusting the wire format completely
evidence: Line 130: const raw: unknown cast as MessagePayload with no validation
Line 135: raw as { messages: MessagePayload[]; hasMore: boolean } -- sync_response
Line 149: raw as MessagePayload -- typed_message
Line 155: raw as { agentId: string; ... } -- agent_trigger_skipped
Line 188-190: raw as { messageId: string; agentId: string; ... } -- stream_start
Lines 211, 220, 244, 280, 295, 307, 319: similar unchecked casts for all stream events
issues.suggestion: Add minimal runtime shape checks for critical payload fields (at least messageId, channelId) before casting. A lightweight guard function would prevent silent failures from malformed gateway messages.

issues.summary: All broadcast functions in gateway-client.ts accept Record<string, unknown> instead of typed payload interfaces, losing type safety at the broadcast boundary
evidence: Lines 69-73: broadcastMessageNew takes payload: Record<string, unknown>
Lines 79-83: broadcastStreamStart takes payload: Record<string, unknown>
Lines 89-93: broadcastStreamToken takes payload: Record<string, unknown>
All 7 broadcast convenience functions use the same untyped signature
issues.suggestion: Define typed interfaces for each broadcast event payload (MessageNewPayload, StreamStartPayload, etc.) matching the PROTOCOL.md contracts. This catches shape mismatches at compile time.

issues.summary: persistMessage accepts PersistMessageData | Record<string, unknown>, allowing callers to bypass the typed interface entirely
evidence: Line 25: data: PersistMessageData | Record<string, unknown> -- the union makes the interface optional
issues.suggestion: Remove the Record<string, unknown> from the union. If callers need to pass additional fields, extend the PersistMessageData interface instead.

issues.summary: Route handlers build updateData as Record<string, unknown> then cast to Prisma types, bypassing compile-time field validation
evidence: Line 156: const createData: Record<string, unknown> = { ... }
Line 172: data: createData as Parameters<typeof tx.message.create>[0]['data']
Line 381: const updateData: Record<string, unknown> = {}
Line 405: data: updateData as Parameters<typeof prismaClient.agent.update>[0]['data']
issues.suggestion: Use Prisma's generated input types (e.g., Prisma.MessageCreateInput) for the data objects instead of Record<string, unknown> with casts.

issues.summary: The BYOK creation path in agents/route.ts POST duplicates the channel-assignment logic that createAgent already handles inside a transaction
evidence: agents/route.ts lines 184-196: manual channel.findMany + channelAgent.createMany outside any transaction
agent-factory.ts lines 101-114: identical channel.findMany + channelAgent.createMany inside a transaction
The BYOK path does NOT use createAgent and lacks transactional consistency
issues.suggestion: Refactor the BYOK creation path to use createAgent (or a shared helper) so channel assignment runs inside the same transaction and logic is not duplicated.

issues.summary: The dispatch route broadcasts before persisting for sync responses but persists before broadcasting for streaming responses
evidence: Lines 148-166: streaming path does persistMessage then broadcastStreamStart (persist-first)
Lines 259-282: sync path does broadcastMessageNew then persistMessage (broadcast-first)
If the broadcast succeeds but persist fails in the sync path, clients see a ghost message
issues.suggestion: Standardize on persist-then-broadcast ordering for all message types in the dispatch route to ensure consistency and prevent ghost messages.

issues.summary: Error messages vary across routes for equivalent failures, making client-side error handling fragile
evidence: servers/route.ts line 74: { error: 'Internal error' }
webhooks/[token]/route.ts line 226: { error: 'Failed to send message' }
agents/messages/route.ts line 254: { error: 'Failed to send message' }
channels/route.ts line 50: { error: 'Internal error' }
v1/agents/[id]/route.ts line 138: { error: 'Update failed' }
issues.suggestion: Define a small set of standardized error codes or messages for common failure modes (e.g., INTERNAL_ERROR, PERSISTENCE_FAILED) and use them consistently across all routes.

issues.summary: internal-api-client.ts throws on errors while route handlers return NextResponse -- two error strategies coexist at the same architectural layer
evidence: internal-api-client.ts line 41: throw new Error('Message persistence failed: ...')
internal-api-client.ts line 71: throw new Error('Message update failed: ...')
All callers must wrap persistMessage/updateMessage calls in try/catch to convert to NextResponse
issues.suggestion: This is an acceptable pattern (throw in helpers, catch in handlers), but document the contract that these functions always throw on failure. The lack of documentation makes the throw-vs-return boundary implicit.

issues.summary: The bootstrap route uses zod schema validation while all other routes perform manual field-by-field validation
evidence: bootstrap/route.ts lines 26-46: uses z.object() with .email(), .min(), .max(), .regex()
agents/route.ts lines 134-136: manual if (!name || typeof name !== 'string' || !name.trim())
servers/route.ts lines 118-131: manual field validation for name and iconUrl
zod is listed as a dependency in package.json but only used in one route
issues.suggestion: Either adopt zod consistently for request body validation across all mutation routes, or remove the zod dependency and use manual validation everywhere. The current mix creates a convention split.

issues.summary: The invites route uses an inline type annotation on its map callback while all other routes avoid this pattern
evidence: invites/route.ts lines 47-55: .map((inv: { id: string; code: string; maxUses: number | null; ... }) => ...) -- a 7-field inline type annotation
No other route handler uses inline type annotations on map callbacks -- they rely on Prisma inference
issues.suggestion: Remove the inline type annotation and let TypeScript infer the type from Prisma's return type, matching the pattern used everywhere else.

issues.summary: use-channel.ts is the most complex client-side module (760 lines, manages WebSocket state, streaming, presence, typing) with zero unit tests
evidence: No test file exists matching use-channel.test.ts in lib/__tests__/
The module handles 12+ distinct Phoenix channel events with complex state management
Stream timeout detection, deduplication, and buffer flushing logic are all untested
issues.suggestion: Add unit tests for the extracted handler functions (registerMessageHandlers, registerStreamingHandlers, etc.) and for the addMessages deduplication logic. These are pure-ish functions that can be tested without a full React rendering context.

issues.summary: gateway-client.ts is called by 6+ route handlers for all real-time broadcasting but has zero test coverage
evidence: No test file exists matching gateway-client.test.ts in lib/__tests__/
The module handles fetch failures, missing env vars, and JSON parsing
fetchChannelSequence has a fallback-to-timestamp behavior that should be verified
issues.suggestion: Add tests for broadcastToChannel error handling (missing secret, non-ok response) and fetchChannelSequence fallback behavior.

issues.summary: API endpoints return inconsistent success shapes: some return {success: true}, others return the entity, others return {ok: true}
evidence: v1/agents/[id] PATCH returns { success: true } (line 135)
v1/agents/[id] DELETE returns { success: true } (line 157)
servers/[serverId] DELETE returns { ok: true } (line 215)
dispatch route returns { ok: true, mode: 'async' | 'sync' | 'stream' } (lines 129, 249, 284, 287)
v1/webhooks DELETE returns { success: true } (line 196)
issues.suggestion: Standardize on a single success shape for non-entity responses. Either always use { success: true } or { ok: true }, not both.

issues.summary: DELETE endpoints inconsistently place the target ID in the URL path vs query parameter vs request body
evidence: v1/agents/{id} DELETE uses the ID from the URL path (standard REST)
v1/webhooks DELETE uses webhookId as a query parameter (line 168)
internal/messages/{messageId} DELETE uses messageId from URL path + userId from body
issues.suggestion: Use URL path parameters for resource identification in DELETE endpoints (e.g., DELETE /api/v1/webhooks/{webhookId}) to match REST conventions used elsewhere.

issues.summary: POST /api/servers/{serverId}/agents does not wrap request.json() in try/catch for the BYOK path, unlike all other mutation routes
evidence: Line 116: const body = await request.json(); -- no try/catch
Compare to v1/webhooks/route.ts lines 26-30: try { body = await request.json() } catch { return 400 }
Compare to v1/agents/[id]/route.ts lines 87-91: same try/catch pattern
An invalid JSON body will cause an unhandled exception returning a 500 instead of a 400
issues.suggestion: Wrap the request.json() call in a try/catch block matching the pattern used in all other mutation route handlers.

issues.summary: Old .js and .test.mjs files are staged for deletion alongside their .ts replacements, indicating the JS-to-TS migration is complete but not yet committed
evidence: Git status shows D packages/web/lib/api-safety.js alongside ?? packages/web/lib/api-safety.ts
D packages/web/lib/route-handlers.js alongside ?? packages/web/lib/route-handlers.ts
D packages/web/lib/validation.js alongside ?? packages/web/lib/validation.ts
Multiple .test.mjs files deleted: validation.test.mjs, route_invocation_safety.test.mjs, etc.
issues.suggestion: Commit the JS-to-TS migration to finalize it. The old files should not linger in the working tree.

issues.summary: PascalCase to kebab-case file rename is partially complete in typed-messages/ and chat/ directories
evidence: Git status shows R (rename): TypedMessageRenderer.tsx -> typed-message-renderer.tsx
R ArtifactRenderer.tsx -> artifact-renderer.tsx
RM MessageMetadata.tsx -> message-metadata.tsx
The index.ts barrel file already uses kebab-case imports, suggesting the migration target is kebab-case
issues.suggestion: Complete the PascalCase to kebab-case rename for all component files and commit it as a single atomic change.

issues.summary: Several comments restate what the code already communicates through naming, adding noise without insight
evidence: agents/route.ts line 161: '// Encrypt the API key' directly above 'const apiKeyEncrypted = encrypt(apiKey)'
socket.ts lines 47-48: socket.onOpen callback with comment '// Connection established -- no action needed' (empty handler)

gateway-client.ts: 6 broadcast wrapper functions each have a JSDoc comment that just restates the function name (e.g., '/** Broadcast a stream_start event to a channel. */' above broadcastStreamStart)
issues.suggestion: Remove comments that restate the function name or the immediately following code. Keep comments that explain WHY or document non-obvious constraints.

issues.summary: ChatProvider has 5 independent refresh functions (refreshServers, refreshChannels, refreshMembers, refreshAgents, refreshPermissions) with identical fetch-parse-setState patterns
evidence: Lines 150-162: refreshServers: fetch + setServers
Lines 164-194: refreshChannels: fetch + setChannels + setCurrentServerName + setCurrentServerOwnerId
Lines 196-221: refreshMembers: fetch + setMembers
Lines 223-250: refreshAgents: fetch + setAgents
Lines 252-274: refreshPermissions: fetch + setUserPermissions + setIsOwner
Each has the same try/catch + console.error structure
issues.suggestion: Extract a shared fetcher helper (e.g., fetchAndSet) that handles the common fetch-check-parse-catch pattern, reducing the boilerplate in each refresh function.

issues.summary: The bootstrap route enforces contracts via zod schema while all other mutation routes use ad-hoc manual validation, creating an inconsistent contract enforcement boundary
evidence: bootstrap/route.ts: bootstrapSchema = z.object({ email: z.string().email(), ... })
agents/route.ts: manual checks like if (!name || typeof name !== 'string' || !name.trim())
channels/route.ts: manual checks like if (!name || name.length < 1 || name.length > 100)
zod dependency is present in package.json but underutilized
issues.suggestion: This creates two contract enforcement strategies. Either extend zod usage to all mutation routes for consistency, or document why bootstrap is special.

issues.summary: ChatProvider manually spreads and preserves unrelated fields when updating serverDataById, creating fragile partial-update logic
evidence: Lines 180-187: setServerDataById(prev => ({ ...prev, [serverId]: { channels: nextChannels, members: prev[serverId]?.members || [], agents: prev[serverId]?.agents || [] } }))
Lines 207-214: same pattern for members update, manually preserving channels and agents
Lines 236-243: same pattern for agents update, manually preserving channels and members
Adding a new field to ServerScopedData requires updating all three spread patterns
issues.suggestion: Use a merging helper: setServerDataById(prev => ({ ...prev, [serverId]: { ...prev[serverId], channels: nextChannels } })) to avoid manually listing all other fields.

issues.summary: Route handler factory functions use overly mechanical names that describe implementation rather than domain intent
evidence: createInternalMessagesPostHandler -- mechanical name describes HTTP method rather than domain action
createInternalMessagesPutHandler -- same pattern
These names read as implementation details rather than domain operations like 'persistMessage' or 'finalizeStream'
issues.suggestion: Rename factory functions to domain-intent names (e.g., createMessagePersistenceHandler, createStreamFinalizationHandler) that communicate what they do rather than which HTTP method they serve.

issues.summary: Gateway event payload types are not defined in @tavok/shared, meaning the Go streaming proxy and web app cannot share a single source of truth for event shapes
evidence: gateway-client.ts uses Record<string, unknown> for all broadcast payloads
use-channel.ts casts raw payloads to local inline types
No shared event payload types exist in packages/shared/types/ for gateway events
PROTOCOL.md defines event shapes in prose but these are not codified as TypeScript types
issues.suggestion: Define gateway event payload interfaces in @tavok/shared so both the broadcast (sender) and handler (receiver) sides reference the same contract.

issues.summary: Several streaming event handlers in use-channel.ts have very long single-line expressions that reduce readability
evidence: Line 299: stream_tool_call handler has a 160+ character line with nested ternary and object spread
Line 220: stream_token handler has complex setMessages callback with nested map and conditional spread
Line 280: stream_complete handler has similar deeply nested update logic
issues.suggestion: Extract the inner message-update logic into small named helper functions (e.g., applyStreamToken, applyStreamComplete) that can be tested independently.

issues.summary: route-handlers.ts contains factory functions for both internal API routes and v1 agent API routes, mixing two different API surface concerns in one module
evidence: createInternalMessagesPostHandler serves /api/internal/messages (internal)
createInternalMessagesPutHandler serves /api/internal/messages/{id} (internal)
Agent PATCH/DELETE handlers serve /api/v1/agents/{id} (v1 public API)
Internal and v1 routes have different auth mechanisms mixed in the same file
issues.suggestion: Consider splitting route-handlers.ts into internal-route-handlers.ts and v1-route-handlers.ts to maintain clear separation between internal and external API surface concerns.

issues.summary: Most modules read environment variables at call time but socket.ts reads NEXT_PUBLIC_GATEWAY_URL at module scope
evidence: socket.ts line 8: const GATEWAY_URL = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_GATEWAY_URL -- read at import time
internal-auth.ts getInternalBaseUrl(): reads env at call time
gateway-client.ts getGatewayHttpUrl(): reads env at call time
Reading at import time means the value is captured once and cannot be reconfigured
issues.suggestion: Move the GATEWAY_URL read inside getSocket() to match the call-time pattern used elsewhere, enabling runtime reconfiguration.

issues.summary: admin-auth.ts is a separate file from agent-auth.ts and internal-auth.ts but serves the same auth concern, creating a scattered auth surface
evidence: Three separate auth files: admin-auth.ts (admin token), agent-auth.ts (API key), internal-auth.ts (internal secret)
Each file handles a different auth type but they share no common abstractions
A developer looking for 'how auth works' must check three separate files
issues.suggestion: Consider creating an auth/ directory to colocate all auth modules, or add a brief index/barrel file that documents the auth surface in one place.

issues.summary: The 7 broadcast convenience wrappers in gateway-client.ts each add only a topic string and event name, providing minimal abstraction value over calling broadcastToChannel directly
evidence: broadcastMessageNew just calls broadcastToChannel(channelId, 'message:new', payload)
broadcastStreamStart just calls broadcastToChannel(channelId, 'stream:start', payload)
All 7 wrappers follow the same 3-line pattern with no additional logic, validation, or type narrowing
issues.suggestion: These wrappers provide naming convenience but no real abstraction. They are acceptable as-is for developer ergonomics, but could be replaced with a typed enum of event names if the list grows further.

issues.summary: zod is included as a dependency but only used in a single route (bootstrap), making it an underutilized dependency
evidence: package.json lists zod as a direct dependency
Only bootstrap/route.ts imports and uses zod
All other routes perform manual validation
issues.suggestion: Either expand zod usage to all mutation routes for consistency, or remove it and use manual validation in bootstrap to eliminate the underutilized dependency.
